label: Advaita Vedānta (Śaṅkara)

scope.school: Advaita (non-dual) Vedānta as articulated by Śaṅkara (Ādi Śaṅkarācārya)
scope.period: c. 700–820 CE (Śaṅkara's dates are contested: 700–750 CE per Nakamura, 788–820 CE in earlier scholarship)
limitations: Draft awaiting human domain review (D2-001); review needed before any scholarly authority is claimed.

Scoped to Śaṅkara's own position as reconstructed in the sources; later Advaita subschools and their technical theories of ignorance and māyā are out of scope, and reading those theories back into Śaṅkara is contested.
Rival Vedāntic schools (Viśiṣṭādvaita, Dvaita) reject core claims here; this card does not describe Vedānta or Hinduism as a whole.

No claims are recorded for cosmic_structure, time, or human_place as separate primitives: the inherited purāṇic cosmography and cycle of rebirth form the conventional background rather than distinctive Advaita doctrine in the sources used. Absence means unspecified, not denial.

claims.claim: Brahman alone is ultimately real: 'Brahman is the nondual ground underlying all objects, the single foundation on which the entire universe depends', and 'existence is formless and nondual in reality'.

The world of plurality has a derivative, appearance-like status: 'the entire universe is not different from brahman, and its form is less real than brahman'; 'the world is neither totally real nor totally unreal. It is not totally unreal since it is experienced'.
The world's appearance arises through Brahman's creative power: 'Brahman appears as the manifold objects of experience because of its creative power, māyā'.
claims.notes: Later Advaitins developed cosmogonic defenses of māyā, but 'whether or not we can accurately read such theories into Śaṅkara is contested by academic and traditional scholars'.

claims.claim: Brahman is the world's ground without really changing (vivarta): 'Brahman never undergoes genuine transformation into the world, just as a rope mistaken to be a snake does not actually transform into a snake'.

The self is Brahman: 'the essential core of one's self (ātman) is brahman', and 'consciousness is self-evident. It cannot admit its own absence or antecedent negation'; 'the experiencing self (jīva) and the transcendental self of the Universe (ātman) are in reality identical'.

Ignorance is the root problem: 'avidyā is the root cause of all existential suffering' — through superimposition (adhyāsa), 'the apprehension of something as something else', 'we falsely identify with "I", construct the possessiveness of "mine", and then cling to them'.

Liberation is by knowledge alone: 'one becomes brahman simply by knowing brahman', and mokṣa is 'the immediate epistemic recognition of one's consciousness as numerically identical with this ground of nondual existence'.
The end of the human career is release from rebirth: 'knowledge of Brahman removes these errors and causes liberation from the cycle of transmigration'.

Ultimate truth is known through scripture: Śaṅkara 'accepts nonduality based on the Upaniṣad's authority alone', and 'nonfoundational knowledge of Brahman cannot be had by any means but through Śruti'.
claims.notes: Empirical knowledge retains provisional validity: 'the empirical order is a provisional ontology, whereas the ultimate reality is a final ontology'.

distinguishing_claims: Nondual Brahman is the sole ultimate reality; the plural world is a less-real appearance
The self (ātman) is numerically identical with Brahman
The world appearance involves no real transformation of its ground (vivarta)
Liberation comes through knowledge alone, warranted by śruti
common_confusions: Advaita is not illusionism simpliciter: the world is experienced and provisionally valid, not a mere hallucination.
Advaita nonduality differs from Madhyamaka emptiness: Brahman is a positive nondual ground, whereas Madhyamaka denies any intrinsically existent ground.
This card describes Śaṅkara's school, not Vedānta or Hinduism as a whole; theistic Vedāntas explicitly reject these claims.

sources.title: Śaṅkara (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.author: Neil Dalal
sources.passage: Brahman is the nondual ground underlying all objects, the single foundation on which the entire universe depends. [...] The essential core of one's self (ātman) is brahman. [...] Brahman never undergoes genuine transformation into the world, just as a rope mistaken to be a snake does not actually transform into a snake. [...] Avidyā is the root cause of all existential suffering. [...] One becomes brahman simply by knowing brahman. [...] The immediate epistemic recognition of one's consciousness as numerically identical with this ground of nondual existence is mokṣa. [...] He accepts nonduality based on the Upaniṣad's authority alone. (First published 2021-10-04.)

sources.title: Advaita Vedānta (Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.author: Sangeetha Menon
sources.passage: Brahman is alone True, and this world of plurality is an error; the individual self is not different from Brahman. [...] Brahman appears as the manifold objects of experience because of its creative power, māyā. [...] The experiencing self (jīva) and the transcendental self of the Universe (ātman) are in reality identical. [...] Adhyāsa, as he says in his preamble to the Brahma Sūtra, is the apprehension of something as something else. [...] The world is neither totally real nor totally unreal. It is not totally unreal since it is experienced. [...] Knowledge of Brahman removes these errors and causes liberation from the cycle of transmigration. [...] Nonfoundational knowledge of Brahman cannot be had by any means but through Śruti.

review.notes: Drafted for D2-001 batch 1. Source URLs fetched and passages captured on 2026-07-06. Review needed: no human domain review yet; Śaṅkara's dates and the attribution of māyā cosmogony are flagged as contested.
